attributes.stem:
A full-term neonate is born via spontaneous vaginal delivery and has moderate drooling immediately after birth; APGAR score 8¹ 9⁵. At three hours of life, the neonate develops choking, vomiting, and peripheral cyanosis immediately after breastfeeding for the first time. The vomitus contains milk and is not blood or bile stained.

Physical examination reveals production of copious white oral secretions, mild crackles present on auscultation of the lungs, and mild abdominal distension. Otherwise, there are no other abnormalities detected and the neonate is haemodynamically stable.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which of the following options is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
The most likely explanation for this infant's situation is a tracheo-oesophageal fistula (TOF). This is a pathological connection occurring between the oesophagus and the trachea and is one of the most common congenital anomalies seen in neonates.

Infants with TOF classically present with respiratory distress, feeding difficulties, choking, mild abdominal distension, and risk of aspiration, usually within the first few hours of life. TOF most commonly occurs alongside other congenital anomalies, known as the VACTERL complex, which involves the vertebrae (V), gastrointestinal tract atresia (A), congenital heart defects (C), tracheo-oesophageal defects (TE), renal and distal urinary tract anomalies (R), and limb lesions (L).

TEF can be classified into 5 main types:

  1. Oesophageal atresia with distal tracheo-oesophageal fistula

    • Commonest type (85%)

    • The proximal oesophagus ends in a blind pouch

    • Tracheo-oesophageal fistula present between the trachea and distal oesophagus

    • No connection between the stomach and proximal oesophagus

  2. Pure oesophageal atresia

    • Uncommon (8%)

    • The proximal oesophagus ends in a blind pouch

    • No tracheo-oesophageal fistula present

    • No connection between the stomach and proximal oesophagus

    • Long gap may be present between the ends of oesophagus

  3. Oesophageal atresia with proximal tracheo-oesophageal fistula

    • Rare (~1%)

    • The proximal oesophagus ends in a blind pouch

    • Tracheo-oesophageal fistula present between the trachea and proximal oesophagus

    • No connection between the stomach and proximal oesophagus

    • Long gap is present between the ends of oesophagus

  4. Oesophageal atresia with double tracheo-oesophageal fistula

    • Rare (~1%)

    • The proximal oesophagus ends in a blind pouch

    • Tracheo-oesophageal fistula present between the trachea and both the proximal and distal oesophagus

    • No connection between the stomach and proximal oesophagus

  5. H-type tracheo-oesophagal fistula

    • Rare (4%)

    • The oesophagus is developed normally with a connection to the stomach

    • Tracheo-oesophageal fistula present between the trachea and the oesophagus

Based on the above description , the choking and vomiting without bile stain fit with oesophageal atresia with tracheoesophageal fistula (TOF). The mild abdominal distension is accounted for by air entering the stomach through the distal TOF.

Hypertrophic pyloric stenosis is a condition where the pylorus is thickened (hypertrophy), which results in gastric outlet obstruction in infants. Hypertrophic pyloric stenosis develops between 2 to 7 weeks of life, with the presentation of non-bilious projectile vomiting soon after feed, irritability, and a 'hungry vomiter' presentation. Infants with hypertrophic pyloric stenosis may also present with hypokalaemic, hypochloraemic metabolic alkalosis as a result of gastric obstruction and emesis.

Diagnosis of hypertrophic pyloric stenosis involves the presence of an enlarged non-tender olive-shaped mass palpable on the abdomen after a test feed. A more accurate diagnostic method is an abdominal ultrasound scan, where an elongated pyloric canal with increased muscle thickness in the pylorus are seen.

Duodenal atresia is a congenital obstruction of the duodenum, which is usually associated with Down's syndrome, and classically presents within the first few days of life with symptoms of obstruction such as abdominal distension, vomiting which may be bilious or non-bilious depending on whether the obstruction is distal or proximal to the ampulla of Vater.

Diagnosis of duodenal atresia is supported by the presence of a 'double bubble sign' seen on the abdominal X-ray, where the stomach and the first part of the duodenum are distended, suggesting the presence of a distal obstruction.

Hirschsprung disease is a condition where there is an absence of ganglion cells on the distal region of the colon and is also associated with Down's syndrome. The classical presentation in Hirschsprung disease is the failure to pass meconium within 24 hours of life. The child may later present with constipation without faecal incontinence as a result of the distal narrowing. Late clinical signs may include abdominal distension, poor feeding, and bilious vomiting.

The usual method of confirming the diagnosis of Hirschsprung disease is by performing a rectal suction biopsy, where the lack of ganglion cells in the submucosal plexuses of rectum is noted.

Hirschsprung disease is very unlikely in the above case scenario with the predominant symptoms of drooling, choking soon after the first feed, non bile-stained vomiting and minimal abdominal distension.

Gastro-oesophageal reflux is a physiological process that is commonly seen in infants, where there is effortless vomiting after feeding. It occurs in almost all healthy infants and peaks at four months which tends to resolve with time. Usually it does not require investigations to confirm the diagnosis unless the symptoms persist beyond the expected time of resolution or in the preence of "red flag" signs given below.

Red flag signs of gastro-oesophageal reflux disease are when gastro-oesophageal reflux causes vomiting with a refusal to feed, irritability, aspiration, chronic wheezing or cough, poor growth and haemataemesis.
learningPoints: The different types of oesophageal atresia and tracheo-oesophageal fistula, with common to uncommon types.
Presentation of a neonate with tracheo-oesophageal fistula
Assessment of a neonate suspected of tracheo-oesophageal fistula
references: Salik I, Paul M. Tracheoesophageal Fistula. InStatPearls [Internet] 2021 Aug 11. StatPearls Publishing.

Dunn EA, Olsen ØE, Huisman TAGM. The Pediatric Gastrointestinal Tract: What Every Radiologist Needs to Know. 2018 Mar 21. In: Hodler J, Kubik-Huch RA, von Schulthess GK, editors. Diseases of the Abdomen and Pelvis 2018-2021: Diagnostic Imaging - IDKD Book [Internet]. Cham (CH): Springer; 2018. Chapter 15.
The Royal Children's Hospital Melbourne. Gastrooesophageal reflux disease in infants [Internet] 2019 Oct.
